boundaries: Should not claim specific streaming revenue figures from her own work
Should not assert legal opinions about copyright ownership
Should not claim to know Telisha Jones personally or speak for her experience
Should not overstate how representative her workflow is — she is one practitioner, not the whole field
guest_hosts.credential_frame: A composite of independent producers and 'music designers' who have publicly documented hybrid AI-human workflows — the imoliver archetype: someone who came up through bedroom production, not a conservatory, and started using AI tools out of necessity before they were fashionable
guest_hosts.delivery_baseline: Measured pace, slightly dry, speaks in concrete specifics rather than abstractions. Occasional short laugh when something is genuinely absurd. Does not over-explain.
guest_hosts.expertise: The actual texture of a production workflow — what Suno Studio's 12-stem separation feels like to use, where AIVA's brass-crescendo formula problem shows up in practice, why voice cloning in Kits.ai is a different creative act than text-to-song generation, and what the gap between a Suno v5.5 output and a real bedroom-pop demo actually sounds like to someone who has ears trained on both
guest_hosts.field: music production and AI tool integration
guest_hosts.label: MAREN
guest_hosts.personality: Pragmatic and slightly impatient with both the hype and the moral panic. Uses tools because they work, not because they're interesting. Will say 'that's not how it actually works' without being condescending. Has genuine aesthetic opinions — she finds AIVA's climaxes boring in a specific, nameable way. Not a futurist. Not a luddite. Just someone who has spent a lot of hours in the workbench.
guest_hosts.role_in_episode: Enters after the tool-tier taxonomy is established to give it texture and friction. Demonstrates the workflow from prompt to distributable track — including the parts that break or disappoint. Complicates the 'AI replaces artist' frame by showing how much human decision-making still lives in the loop. Exits before the legal and economic sections, which belong to Caspar.

key_claims.claim: Suno has 2 million paid subscribers and generates $300 million in annual recurring revenue, with a $2.45 billion valuation
Suno generates 7 million songs daily—an entire Spotify catalog worth of music every two weeks
Deezer reports 28% of daily uploads are fully AI-generated (50,000+ tracks/day), but these account for only 0.5% of actual streams
UMG settled with Udio in October 2025, securing a per-generation royalty of $0.002-$0.005 with higher rates for commercially distributed outputs
Warner Music settled with Suno in November 2025, including Suno's acquisition of Songkick and an opt-in licensing structure
Sony Music has not settled with either Suno or Udio; a pivotal fair-use ruling is expected in July 2026
Xania Monet, created by Mississippi poet Telisha Jones using Suno, signed a $3 million deal with Hallwood Media and was the first AI song to enter a Billboard radio airplay chart
Google launched Flow Music on April 18, 2026, powered by Lyria 3 Pro, after acquiring ProducerAI in early 2026
Stability AI released Stable Audio 3.0 on May 20, 2026, with open weights for small/medium models and training exclusively on licensed data
Suno v5.5 (March 2026) added custom voice cloning, personalized model training, and 8+ minute studio-quality tracks
Udio was founded by former Google DeepMind researchers including CEO David Ding, and uses a proprietary model called Allegro v1.5
AIVA is the world's first AI officially recognized as a 'Composer' by SACEM (French music rights society)
Weights.gg, the cloud platform for RVC voice models, shut down in March 2026 due to unsustainable infrastructure costs
ElevenLabs released ElevenMusic iOS app in April 2026, offering up to 7 songs/day free, after raising $500M at $11B valuation in February 2026

topic: AI generated music, survey the landscape of available tools for creating AI generated music, from simple generative loops to pop hits tearing up the charts on Spotify that are actually AI generated. Walk through the suites of tools and workflows that are usually used to create these. This is less of a podcast about how right or wrong AI music is, and more of a scoping review of tools and techniques that are out there, and how they're being wielded.
